title: DNA — the molecule of inheritance
examTip: DNA structure questions are reliable marks — AQA regularly asks you to describe the double helix, define gene vs genome vs chromosome, and state benefits of the Human Genome Project. Get those definitions pinned down and you won't lose easy marks.
introTips: Everything that makes you you — your height, your eye colour, whether you're prone to certain diseases — comes down to one molecule: DNA. Understanding its structure tells you why it works so well as an information store.
The key shift in this topic is moving from the big picture (chromosomes you can see under a microscope) down to the molecular level (bases pairing up in a helix). Both scales matter for the exam.
The Human Genome Project is a guaranteed exam area — make sure you can state at least two medical benefits and explain why mapping genes matters for medicine.
intro: Your body contains about 37 trillion cells, and almost every single one holds the complete instruction manual for building you. That manual is your DNA — two metres of it, coiled up inside each nucleus. Understanding DNA isn't just biology for its own sake: it's why we can test for genetic diseases before birth, why we can design drugs that target specific proteins, and why forensic science works. This lesson covers the structure of the molecule, the language it uses, and what the Human Genome Project has done for medicine.

keyPoints.heading: The structure of DNA — the double helix
keyPoints.content: DNA is a polymer made of repeating units called nucleotides. Each nucleotide has three parts: a sugar (deoxyribose), a phosphate group, and one of four bases — Adenine (A), Thymine (T), Cytosine (C), or Guanine (G). Two strands of nucleotides twist around each other to form a double helix. The two strands are held together by complementary base pairing: A always pairs with T, and C always pairs with G. This rule (A-T, C-G) is non-negotiable and is the basis of how DNA copies itself accurately.
writeBullets: DNA is a polymer of nucleotides
Each nucleotide: deoxyribose sugar + phosphate + one base (A, T, C or G)
Two strands twisted into a double helix
Bases pair: A with T, C with G (complementary base pairing)
Base pairing rule is the key to accurate DNA replication

keyTerms.def: The monomer unit of DNA — consists of a sugar, a phosphate group, and a base
The twisted-ladder shape of the DNA molecule, with two strands running antiparallel
The rule that A always bonds with T, and C always bonds with G
One of four molecules (A, T, C, G) whose sequence encodes genetic information

keyPoints.memTip: AT the GC — Adenine pairs with Thymine, Guanine pairs with Cytosine. Say it as a phrase: 'AT the GC' (like 'at the gym'). You'll never mix them up again.

keyPoints.heading: Genes, chromosomes and the genome
keyPoints.content: A gene is a short section of DNA that codes for a specific protein. The sequence of bases in the gene determines which protein is made. Human body cells contain 23 pairs of chromosomes (46 total). Each chromosome is one very long, tightly coiled DNA molecule. A genome is the complete set of genetic information in an organism — all the DNA in all the chromosomes. The human genome contains roughly 20,000–25,000 genes, but most of the DNA between genes does not code for proteins.
writeBullets: Gene = section of DNA that codes for one protein
Humans have 23 pairs of chromosomes in body cells (46 total)
Each chromosome = one long DNA molecule, tightly coiled
Genome = all the DNA in an organism
Human genome contains ~20,000–25,000 genes; most DNA is non-coding

keyTerms.def: A section of DNA that codes for a specific protein
A long, coiled DNA molecule found in the nucleus; humans have 46 (23 pairs)
The complete set of genetic information in an organism — all its DNA
DNA sequences that do not code for proteins — makes up the majority of the human genome

keyPoints.memTip: Think of it as zoom levels: Genome (all the books) → Chromosomes (individual books) → Genes (chapters) → Base sequence (the words). Each level contains the one below it.

keyPoints.heading: The Human Genome Project — mapping our genes
keyPoints.content: The Human Genome Project (HGP) was an international scientific collaboration completed in 2003. Its goal was to sequence all 3 billion base pairs in the human genome and identify every gene. The medical benefits are significant: scientists can now identify genes associated with specific diseases (such as BRCA1 and breast cancer risk), which allows for genetic testing before symptoms appear. It also enables the development of personalised medicines — drugs tailored to a person's specific genetic variants. Understanding which proteins are coded for by which genes opens up entirely new approaches to treating inherited conditions.
writeBullets: HGP completed in 2003 — sequenced all ~3 billion base pairs in human DNA
Identified the location and sequence of every human gene
Allows genetic testing: find disease-linked genes before symptoms appear
Enables personalised medicines: drugs tailored to individual genetic make-up
Helps scientists understand inherited conditions and develop new treatments

keyTerms.def: International project that sequenced the entire human genome — all 3 billion base pairs — completed in 2003
Testing DNA to identify genes associated with increased disease risk
Medical treatment tailored to an individual's specific genetic make-up

keyPoints.memTip: HGP benefits: MAP (Medical testing, Adapted/personalised medicines, Proteins understood). Three benefits, one acronym. Examiners award marks for specific examples — name BRCA1 for extra credit.

tables.title: DNA — key terms compared
rows: Monomer unit of DNA (sugar + phosphate + base)
Section of DNA coding for one protein
All the DNA in an organism

1.question: Which base pairing rule is correct for DNA?
options: A pairs with G, C pairs with T
A pairs with C, G pairs with T
A pairs with T, C pairs with G
A pairs with A, C pairs with C
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — A always pairs with T, and C always pairs with G. This complementary base pairing rule is the foundation of how DNA copies itself accurately.
1.wrongFeedback: The base pairing rule is A-T and C-G. A memory trick: AT the GC. Adenine with Thymine, Cytosine with Guanine. The bases are complementary — they only fit with their specific partner.

2.question: What is a gene?
options: The complete set of all DNA in an organism
A single nucleotide in the DNA strand
A section of DNA that codes for a specific protein
One of the 46 chromosomes in a human body cell
2.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — a gene is a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein. The sequence of bases in that section determines which protein is produced.
2.wrongFeedback: A gene is a section of DNA that codes for a specific protein. The complete set of all DNA is the genome. A nucleotide is a single monomer unit. A chromosome is the whole DNA molecule — it contains thousands of genes.

3.question: Which of the following is a medical benefit of the Human Genome Project?
options: It allows scientists to create new species by rewriting genomes
It enables genetic testing to identify disease-linked genes before symptoms appear
It allows doctors to remove harmful genes from patients directly
It proved that all humans have identical DNA sequences
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Good — genetic testing using HGP data allows scientists to identify genes linked to diseases like breast cancer (BRCA1) before symptoms develop, enabling earlier intervention.
3.wrongFeedback: The Human Genome Project mapped all human genes, which enables genetic testing — identifying disease-linked genes before symptoms appear. It also supports personalised medicine. It did not create new species or directly edit genes — that's a separate technology.

commonMistakes: Confusing gene, chromosome, and genome — these are three different scales. A gene is a section of DNA; a chromosome is a whole DNA molecule containing many genes; the genome is all the chromosomes together.
Getting the base pairing wrong — A pairs with T (not C), and C pairs with G (not A). The pairs are complementary, not identical.
Saying the Human Genome Project found a cure for genetic diseases — it didn't. It mapped the genes, enabling testing and personalised medicine, but it didn't directly cure anything.
Forgetting that humans have 23 pairs (46 total) chromosomes in body cells — gametes have 23 single chromosomes (haploid).
Describing DNA as being 'inside genes' — it's the other way around. Genes are made of DNA; DNA is the molecule, genes are sections of it.
examTips: When asked to describe DNA structure, include: double helix, two strands of nucleotides, complementary base pairing (A-T, C-G). Missing the base pairing rule often loses a mark.
Distinguish clearly: gene = codes for one protein; chromosome = one long DNA molecule; genome = all the DNA. AQA uses all three terms in questions and expects precision.

For Human Genome Project questions, give two specific benefits: (1) genetic testing to identify disease-linked genes, (2) development of personalised medicines. Vague answers like 'helps with medicine' score poorly.

revisionCardBullets: DNA is a polymer of nucleotides — each nucleotide has a sugar, phosphate, and base
Four bases: A, T, C, G — base pairing rule: A-T and C-G (complementary)
DNA forms a double helix — two strands twisted together
Gene = section of DNA that codes for one specific protein
Chromosome = one long, coiled DNA molecule — humans have 46 (23 pairs) in body cells
Genome = all the DNA in an organism — the complete genetic information
Human Genome Project (completed 2003) — sequenced all ~3 billion base pairs
HGP benefits: genetic testing for disease-linked genes + personalised medicines
Most DNA does not code for proteins — only gene sequences do
Gametes have 23 chromosomes (haploid); body cells have 46 (diploid)
